FHIR patient outcomes export

Patient Health Outcomes Understanding And Recommendations

Historical population analysis for understanding how patient measures changed over time, which diagnosis-linked cohorts improved, and what care capability investments should be prioritized next.

Summary Framework Data Model Hypotheses Analysis Recommendations Future Work Appendix

Executive Summary

Findings

  • Outcome change is selective. Strongest improvement signal is Chronic Pain / Pain score at 70.8% (24 eligible patients). Weaker movement appears in Obesity / BMI at 25.0%, which is a better target for workflow review than a success claim.
  • Hypertension has a measurable BP improvement signal. Diagnosis-anchored systolic and diastolic BP both improve for repeated-measure patients, but treatment attribution still needs controls.
  • Kidney and pain signals are promising but need clinical interpretation. Renal labs and pain scores move favorably, yet renal values can reflect acute episodes, dialysis, or measurement artifacts.
  • Behavioral health and obesity need follow-up. PHQ-2 and BMI move less clearly, suggesting care-process or measurement-definition gaps.

Recommendations

  • Prioritize care capability around hypertension/BP, diabetes/glucose, kidney labs, chronic pain, obesity/BMI, and behavioral-health screening.
  • Use age and diagnosis cohorts to identify follow-up populations rather than relying on aggregate population averages.
  • Treat exposure findings as hypotheses. Medication, procedure, and care-management differences are useful prioritization signals, not causal proof.
  • Invest in data depth. Add more patients, expert-reviewed outcome definitions, A1c, medication adherence/claims, and systematic controls. Current hypothesis status: 2 validated, 6 need follow-up, 0 rejected.

1. Analysis High-Level Framework

  • Question: how have patient health outcomes changed over time, after anchoring outcomes to the health problems we can observe in the FHIR export?
  • Answer so far: change is selective rather than universal. Diagnosis-anchored improvement is strongest where the diagnosis-to-measure link is direct and repeated measurement exists.
  • Why this flow: it first builds a reproducible, high-rigor analytics framework and data model, then uses that model to inform business decisions while making investment gaps visible, especially expert-defined outcomes and more patient data.
Patient
What health problem do they have?
What treatment did we provide?
How did measurements change after diagnosis?
Did outcomes improve over time?

Baseline rule update: for scoped diseases, first value now means the first valid numeric measurement on or after the first qualifying diagnosis date. Body weight, heart rate, and respiratory rate remain general first-measurement baselines because they are broad monitoring signals rather than disease-specific outcomes.

Patients120
Encounters9,069
Measurement events21,385
Treatment events26,478
Patient-measure outcomes1,098
Hypotheses mapped8

2. Data Exploration And Modeling

The model starts from raw local FHIR NDJSON and converts patients, diagnoses, measurements, treatments, and procedures into reusable analytics tables. The new diagnosis-anchored tables separate disease-specific outcomes from general monitoring measures so Tableau and future notebooks can use the same definitions.

Patient outcomes analytics data model: the ERD below shows how raw FHIR resources flow into patient and measure dimensions, clinical facts, diagnosis-anchored outcome facts, treatment bridges, and final analysis marts.

Which data supports each stage

Analysis StageQuestion AnsweredRaw Fhir SourcesProcessed Tables
1. Patient populationWho are the patients and when do they enter care?Patient.ndjson; Encounter.ndjsondim_patient_master; fact_encounter
2. Health problemsWhat diagnoses or issues do they have?Condition.ndjsonfact_condition_event; mart_diagnosis_measure_scope
3. Treatment and care exposureWhat medications, procedures, immunizations, devices, or care-management signals ...MedicationRequest.ndjson; Procedure.ndjson; Immunization.ndjson; DeviceRequest.nd...fact_treatment_event; fact_procedure_event; bridge_measure_treatment_window
4. MeasurementsHow did measurements change from baseline to latest?Observation.ndjsondim_measure; fact_measurement_event; fact_patient_measure_outcome; fact_patient_d...
5. Diagnosis-anchored outcomesAfter a qualifying diagnosis, did the relevant outcome measure improve?Condition.ndjson + Observation.ndjsonfact_patient_diagnosis_measure_outcome; mart_diagnosis_measure_improvement_summar...
6. Hypotheses and recommendationsWhich hypotheses are supported, rejected, or need follow-up, and what should the ...All scoped raw FHIR resourcesmart_hypothesis_decisions; mart_treatment_association_by_issue_age; report figure...

Dim, fact, bridge, and mart tables

Model OutputGrainDescription
dim_patient_masterone row per patientPatient demographics, problem flags, care exposure rollups, outcome rollups.

fact_condition_eventone row per conditionHealth problems and clinical-domain flags.

fact_procedure_eventone row per procedure eventProcedure-specific care exposure fact.
fact_patient_measure_outcomeone row per patient + measureFirst/latest/change/meaningful-improvement outcome fact.

mart_improver_exposure_comparisonmeasure + age cohort + exposure featureH5/H6/H8 validation mart.
mart_hypothesis_validation_planone row per hypothesisHypothesis review plan mapped to available data.
mart_hypothesis_decisionsone row per hypothesisReport-facing approve/reject/needs-follow-up status and rationale.
mart_metric_definitionsone row per measureReport-facing metric, improver, and non-improver definitions.
mart_measurement_trend_by_agemeasure + age cohort + quarter since first measureTrend chart data by age group.
mart_measurement_trend_by_entry_cohortmeasure + first-measure calendar bucket + quarter since first measureTrend chart data by user/entry cohort.
mart_findings_summaryone row per finding/story pointReport-ready finding, recommendation, strength, and caveat.
mart_diagnosis_measure_scopeone row per diagnosis scope + condition display + measureDefines which diagnoses are clinically tied to which measures and what baseline r...
fact_patient_diagnosis_measure_outcomeone row per patient + diagnosis/general scope + measureFirst value after diagnosis or general first measurement, latest value, change, a...
mart_diagnosis_measure_improvement_summaryone row per diagnosis/general scope + measureImprovement rates and median first/latest values from diagnosis-anchored or gener...
mart_diagnosis_measure_trend_after_anchorone row per diagnosis/general scope + measure + quarter since anchorTrend-ready median measurement values after diagnosis anchor or general baseline.

Concise definitions and outcome scope

Improver: latest value after diagnosis moved from first value after diagnosis by at least the measure-specific threshold in the favorable direction. Non-improver: stable, worsened, or improved less than the threshold. General monitoring: body weight, heart rate, and respiratory rate use first valid measurement as baseline and are not scored as universal improvement.

3. Hypotheses We Want To Explore

The hypothesis layer turns EDA into decision questions. Approved findings can guide immediate monitoring; needs-follow-up findings should guide better controls, better data capture, or expert review before becoming care policy.

Hypothesis IdHypothesisValidation MethodDecision StatusCurrent Report EvidenceDecision RationaleKey Caveat
H1Recent patients receive more protocolized careCompare care-management and screening rates by first-measure calendar bucketNeeds follow-upProtocolized care is more visible in recent cohorts, but documentation/workflow c...Exploratory descriptive output generated; final approve/reject requires reviewing...Documentation changes may explain differences
H2Clinical outcome trajectories differ across age cohortsCompare improvement rates and trajectories by age cohortApproveAge-cohort cuts in the diagnosis-measure marts show different denominators and im...Current descriptive model shows material variation by age cohort and clinical dom...Sparse cohorts reduce confidence
H3Improvement rates differ across clinical domainsCompare meaningful improvement rates across outcome familiesApproveDiagnosis-anchored improvement rates range from 25.0% to 70.8% across analysis-re...Current descriptive model shows material variation by age cohort and clinical dom...Measurement frequency differs across domains
H4Renal outcome improvement varies by age cohortCompare eGFR, creatinine, BUN by age cohortNeeds follow-upRenal outcomes show improvement on creatinine and BUN, but eGFR has too few repea...Exploratory descriptive output generated; final approve/reject requires reviewing...Severity/treatment intensity confounds results
H5Cardiovascular care exposure is associated with BP improvementCompare BP improvers vs non-improvers within age cohortsNeeds follow-upDiagnosis-anchored BP outcomes improve for hypertension patients, but treatment e...Associations can be described, but require adjusted or matched analysis before st...Association is not causal
H6Behavioral-health engagement is associated with behavioral-health improvementCompare PHQ-2 improvers vs non-improvers by screening/care-management exposureNeeds follow-upPHQ-2 improvement exists for a subset, but median PHQ-2 remains unchanged; screen...Associations can be described, but require adjusted or matched analysis before st...Screening may be triggered by higher risk
H7Calendar-era improvement is selective, not universalCompare improvement rates by measure/domain/calendar bucketNeeds follow-upCalendar-era improvement appears selective and may be mixed with documentation ch...Calendar-era signals appear selective and mixed; report should classify measure-s...Recent cohorts may have shorter follow-up
H8Greater care engagement predicts better outcomesCompare engagement intensity to composite/domain improvementNeeds follow-upEngagement plausibly predicts outcomes, but encounter and measurement density can...Associations can be described, but require adjusted or matched analysis before st...Engagement may proxy acuity/access

4. Diagnosis And Measure Detailed Analysis

This is the core outcome analysis: for each scoped disease and measure, compare the first value after diagnosis with the latest observed value after diagnosis, then calculate meaningful improvement rates when the measure has a directional threshold.

269 repeated diagnosis-anchored patient-measure rows are available for scoped diseases. 360 repeated general-baseline rows are available for body weight, heart rate, and respiratory rate monitoring.

Diagnosis-model insights: chronic pain and kidney labs show the highest analysis-ready improvement rates, hypertension shows meaningful BP improvement after diagnosis, diabetes/glucose is mixed, and obesity/BMI plus behavioral-health screening show lower or flatter median movement. Renal lab improvements need clinical review because creatinine and BUN can reflect acute episodes or dialysis-related measurement patterns; eGFR is too sparse for a decision-ready claim.

Trend after diagnosis or general baseline

The trend view shows whether median values move over patient journey time after the anchor. Diagnosis-scoped measures use first diagnosis as the anchor; body weight and general vitals use first measurement as the anchor.

Improvement rate by disease and age

Age matters for operational targeting. The tables below keep disease first, then age, so care teams can identify which disease-age combinations have enough sample and which outcomes are improving.

Treatment and care exposure signals

Treatment associations remain observational but useful for prioritization. Positive differences mean exposed patients had higher improver rates than unexposed patients within the same issue and age context.

745 analysis-ready treatment rows show a positive exposed-vs-unexposed improver-rate difference.

5. Recommendations

Use the diagnosis-anchored model to prioritize care capability where the population has both patient volume and measurable outcomes. The first focus should be conditions that are common in the export, have repeated measures, and show room for improvement by age cohort.

  • Care capability prioritization: focus near-term clinical programs on hypertension/BP, obesity/BMI, diabetes/glucose, kidney disease labs, chronic pain scores, and behavioral-health screening because these now have explicit diagnosis-to-measure definitions.
  • Age-specific follow-up: use mart_diagnosis_measure_improvement_by_age.csv to find age cohorts with enough denominator and lower improvement rates, then design follow-up workflows for those disease-age groups.
  • User acquisition and onboarding: identify incoming patients whose diagnoses match high-volume scopes and immediately establish baseline measurement capture after diagnosis.
  • Care-management operations: use treatment exposure signals as hypothesis queues for clinical review, not as proof that a medication or procedure caused improvement.
  • Dashboard build: load the diagnosis-anchored outcome fact, trend mart, treatment association mart, and patient dimension into Tableau so teams can filter by disease, age, measure, and analysis-ready status.

Kidney DiseaseCreatinine201470.0%36.02.0-3.3True

6. Potential Future Analysis

The next investment is rigor and measurement quality. The current work builds the reproducible frame; the next phase should improve causal confidence and clinical validity.

  • Systematic controls for causality: add propensity weighting, matched cohorts, or doubly robust models using age, sex, baseline value, diagnosis burden, encounter density, and observation frequency.
  • Expert-defined disease outcomes: review thresholds and diagnosis-to-measure definitions with clinicians; for example, glucose is useful, but A1c would likely be better for diabetes control.
  • More data: 120 patients is enough for a rigorous prototype but too small for many disease-age-treatment intersections, especially eGFR and rare diagnoses.
  • More complete exposures: add care-plan details, adherence, medication fill data, claims, and payer utilization to distinguish documented treatment from received treatment.
  • Documentation drift checks: test whether recent cohorts look better because care improved or because newer workflows captured more screenings, assessments, and reconciliations.

Key caveat: this report is observational. Diagnosis anchoring improves clinical interpretability, but it does not remove confounding from disease severity, access, engagement, or documentation density.

The main report keeps definitions concise; this appendix preserves the full audit detail. Use these tables when reviewing exact diagnosis displays, measurement thresholds, or baseline rules.
